# 1. Bibliographic Metadata

**Full citation (APA 7)**
Ting, D. S. W., Peng, L., Varadarajan, A. V., Keane, P. A., Burlina, P. M., Chiang, M. F., … Wong, T. Y. (2019). Deep learning in ophthalmology: The technical and clinical considerations. *Progress in Retinal and Eye Research, 72*, 100759.

**DOI:** 10.1016/j.preteyeres.2019.04.003

**Journal (+ publisher):** Progress in Retinal and Eye Research (Elsevier)

**Year:** 2019

**Publication type:** Review / Survey

**Research domain classification:** Deep learning, ophthalmology, clinical translation.

**Justification:** Authoritative ophthalmology-DL review for §1.3.1/§1.4/§6.3 framing.

---

# 3. Research Problem

Surveys technical foundations (CNNs, datasets, evaluation) and clinical considerations (deployment, regulation, pitfalls) of DL in ophthalmology, esp. DR/glaucoma/AMD. Addresses **field landscape + clinical translation**.

---

# 4. Datasets Used

N/A (survey of public/clinical datasets).

---

# 5. Preprocessing Pipeline

Reviews image preprocessing/quality as a translation consideration.

---

# 6. Model Architecture

Reviews CNN architectures and pipelines used in ophthalmic DL.

---

# 7. Validation Design

N/A — discusses validation best practices (external validation, reference standards, generalizability).

---

# 8. Performance Metrics

N/A — synthesizes reported performance and pitfalls (overfitting, dataset shift, black-box concerns).

---

# 9. Authors' Claims

DL shows strong ophthalmic-diagnostic performance but clinical translation requires external validation, interpretability, regulatory compliance, and workflow integration.

---

# 10. Empirical Support Assessment

Coverage-based; authoritative synthesis.

---

Survey scope; expert-authored.

Broad translational guidance.

# 16. Citation-Ready Statements

1. Deep learning has achieved robust diagnostic performance in DR, glaucoma, and AMD screening, but requires careful technical and clinical validation. (Abstract)
2. External validation, interpretability, and workflow integration are key to clinical translation. (Clinical considerations)

---

# 17. Epistemic Classification

**Review / Survey (ophthalmology DL).** High-authority venue.

---

# 18. Analytical Synthesis

Ting et al. is an authoritative, high-impact review that frames the dissertation's §1.3.1/§1.4 field positioning and its §6.3 deployment chapter, emphasizing the external-validation, interpretability, and workflow-integration imperatives that the dissertation operationalizes (cross-dataset experiments, Grad-CAM explainability, system-architecture design). It contributes no primary metrics and predates foundation models, so it serves as translational framing rather than current SOTA; it is preprocessing-supportive in noting image quality as a translation factor but neutral on the specific preprocessing-dominance test.

End of Literature Card.
